reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: organized-labour interest in informed, participatory policy-making and protection against uncosted implementation burden; union concern that audits without teacher-cost assessment become unfunded policy mandates
reactions.interest: representing employed teachers' interest in not bearing invisible implementation costs; advocating for union input into any reform triggered by audit findings
reactions.public_good_frame: teacher voice in policy design improves implementation feasibility and institutional credibility; audit credibility depends on transparent assessment of what teaching-staff retraining and transition will actually require
reactions.argument: audit-plus-deadline mechanism is acceptable if audit scope explicitly includes teacher-implementation cost assessment (retraining time/cost, class-size management during transition, workload increase risk), and if any resulting reform is front-loaded with binding union consultation rights and guaranteed retraining funding. Audit without teacher perspective risks prescribing solutions that are administratively elegant but pedagogically unrealistic or uncosted. Union condition: audit TOR must include teacher-feasibility and transition-cost assessment as a dimension, not afterthought.
reactions.condition_to_change: union opposes if audit is framed as 'technical' and excludes teacher voice from design; opposes any resulting reform process that treats retraining/transition as an unfunded expectation; would support if audit explicitly models teacher-implementation burden and any resulting reform negotiates transition costs with union before legislative tabling

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: organized-labour interest in workload management and professional autonomy; union concern that pilots are often unfunded and teachers absorb design and implementation costs invisibly
reactions.interest: representing teachers in pilot districts' interest in resources, workload protection, and professional input into assessment redesign
reactions.public_good_frame: pilots that include teacher voice and adequate resourcing generate credible evidence about feasibility; pilots that impose workload costs on teachers without resources discredit the intervention with the workforce and create risk of gaming (teachers withdrawing effort if unsupported)
reactions.argument: SES-aware admission pilot is conditionally acceptable if: (a) pilot-district teachers are granted union participation in assessment-redesign working groups (not post-hoc consultation), (b) pilot schools receive demonstrable resource increase (e.g., class-size reduction if SES weighting increases compositional heterogeneity), (c) any new assessment development includes teacher time and external expertise, not added to existing teaching load, (d) union has binding (not advisory) role in mid-pilot feasibility review and timeline adjustment. Selective-track pilots historically concentrate high-achieving pupils with adequate resource; SES weighting that increases heterogeneity without proportional resources increases workload risk for pilot-school teachers.
reactions.condition_to_change: union supports if pilot is fully resourced and teacher-designed; opposes if pilot is framed as 'no additional cost' or imposes assessment change without consultation or workload adjustment; would shift to support if government commits to scaling resources proportional to any compositional changes detected in pilot data

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: organized-labour interest in job security, voluntary mobility, and avoiding forced redeployment; union concern that phase-down of selective entry without binding protections will force gymnasium-trained teachers into general schools against preference, and will increase general-school class sizes without compensatory funding given Hungary's documented teacher shortage
reactions.interest: representing gymnasium teachers' interest in security and autonomy; representing general-school teachers' interest in workload and class-size protection
reactions.public_good_frame: forced redeployment without consent erodes professional morale and retention; uncompensated workload increase (larger classes in general schools) accelerates teacher attrition in already-shortage conditions; credible reform requires binding protections, not administrative assurances
reactions.argument: phase-down without guarantees risks: (1) forced moves of gymnasium teachers trained for selective cohorts into general classes; (2) class-size increases in general schools if phase-down shrinks gymnasium places but new 'retained/displaced' pupils are absorbed without additional funding; (3) acceleration of attrition given Hungary's documented systemic teacher shortage (OECD, PSZ/PDSZ data). Union requirement for conditional support: (a) no forced redeployment—gymnasium teachers retain right to exit or retrain voluntarily with funded retraining program; (b) general schools absorbing displaced pupils receive demonstrable capacity investment (new positions or class-size caps, verified in advance, not promised post-hoc); (c) non-state maintainer place expansion is legally capped or monitored so offsetting does not undermine the state-side reduction. Without binding guarantees on all three, phase-down is workload transfer in shortage conditions.
reactions.condition_to_change: union would shift to conditional support only if government legislates (not administratively commits to) binding guarantees on redeployment voluntariness (with union arbitration over disputes), class-size protections (e.g., statutory cap on general-school class sizes during phase-down period), and regulatory instruments to limit non-state offsetting expansion. A multi-year agreement co-signed with union leadership would be necessary, not a ministry working group.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: organized-labour interest in job security, professional autonomy, and avoidance of high-risk redeployment under implementation-capacity constraints; union concern that abolition-scale retraining in shortage conditions is unrealistic and will result in forced moves, workload increase, and attrition
reactions.interest: representing gymnasium teachers' interest in security and autonomy; representing all teachers' interest in sustainable implementation conditions; broader union interest in not being forced to absorb structural reform risks that government cannot adequately resource
reactions.public_good_frame: structural abolition without adequate teacher-supply infrastructure risks producing underfunded, poorly-trained comprehensive classrooms worse than current sorted system; credible reform requires realistic implementation timelines and union partnership, not legislative imposition
reactions.argument: abolition requires gymnasium-teacher retraining and redeployment at scale (exact numbers unknown, as scenario concedes) in a context of documented systemic teacher shortage in mathematics, sciences, and rural regions (OECD Education at a Glance; PSZ/PDSZ). Scenario's own implementation_planning concedes full national teacher-capacity build in 4 years is 'unlikely given current shortage levels.' Union position: abolition as currently specified is not implementable without forced redeployment and workload increase; union opposes unilateral structural change under these conditions. If government proposes abolition despite shortage, union demands: (a) 7-year minimum phase-in (not 4-5), (b) union-led (not government-led) retraining program design with external funding and voluntary participation; teachers exiting gymnasium choose retraining or exit with enhanced severance, not forced moves; (c) statutory class-size caps for new comprehensive classes (e.g., max 20-22 pupils) during transition to prevent workload substitution for structural change; (d) binding, enforceable union veto over implementation pacing if teacher shortage worsens during phase-in; (e) multi-party pact including union as co-signatory with binding enforcement mechanism (not advisory). Current proposal lacks all five.
reactions.condition_to_change: union would shift from oppose to conditional support only if government commits in legislation (not ministerial memo): 7-year phase-in minimum, union-led retraining design with full external funding, voluntary redeployment with severance option, class-size statutory caps, and union co-signature on any multi-party pact with enforceable veto over pace if shortage metrics worsen. Without all five, abolition is unacceptable risk transfer to teachers.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: organized-labour interest in resources for members, professional autonomy in curriculum implementation, and avoidance of unfunded mandates; union support for targeted funding that translates to hiring/workload reduction rather than expectation that teachers absorb support functions
reactions.interest: representing general-school teachers' interest in workload, resources, and class-size management; advocating for union input into curriculum-standards design and grade-retention-reduction implementation
reactions.public_good_frame: curriculum reform and targeted support for disadvantaged schools require teacher buy-in and adequate resources to succeed; reforms imposed without teacher voice or adequately funded become workplace resentment and ineffective implementation; durability of TEIP-style funding depends on multi-year political commitment and union accountability partnership
reactions.argument: non-structural package is conditionally acceptable if: (a) national curriculum-standards development includes union participation in design (not post-publication consultation); standards are binding on schools, not imposed on individual teachers as workload increase without support; (b) TEIP-style targeted funding is legislatively committed for 5+ years (not annual budget discretion) and is sized to translate to teacher hiring or class-size reduction, not absorbed into administrative costs; union has auditing rights to verify funding reaches classroom level; (c) grade-retention-reduction support is resourced adequately—remedial/diagnostic functions are assigned to specialized staff (pedagogical aide, special educator, counselor), not added to individual teachers' teaching load; (d) implementation timeline acknowledges teacher shortage (OECD data) and sequences rollout by adequately-staffed regions first, not nationally uniform; (e) union is consulted on any curriculum assessment changes triggered by standards. S4 avoids the redeployment risks of S2/S3, but only delivers equity if funding is durable and teacher workload is protected.
reactions.condition_to_change: union supports if curriculum standards are co-designed and targeted funding is legislatively secured multi-year; opposes if standards are imposed without consultation or if targeted funding is presented as unfunded mandate ('implement with existing budget'); would shift from conditional to strong support if government commits statutory ring-fencing of targeted funding and union auditing access; would shift to opposition if funding is cut or if shortage worsens and implementation timeline is not adjusted downward in response
